entries.title: Trinity Church, Boston
entries.description: Completed in 1877, Trinity Church's Romanesque Revival tower stands 211 feet tall and remains one of the finest examples of H.H. Richardson's architectural genius in New England.

entries.title: Old North Church, Boston
entries.description: Famous for the two lanterns hung in its steeple to warn of British troop movements in 1775, this 1723 Anglican church boasts the oldest church bells still hung in their original configuration in North America.

entries.title: St. Michael's Church, Charleston
entries.description: Built between 1752 and 1761, St. Michael's steeple rises 186 feet above Meeting Street and has guided sailors into Charleston Harbor for over two centuries.

entries.title: First Congregational Church, Litchfield
entries.description: This classic New England white-clapboard church from 1829 features a pristine three-tiered steeple that has become an enduring symbol of Connecticut's colonial heritage.

entries.title: Cathedral of the Madeleine, Salt Lake City
entries.description: The twin Gothic Revival towers of this 1909 cathedral soar 210 feet into the Utah sky, their intricate stonework a striking contrast to the rugged Wasatch Mountains beyond.

entries.title: St. John the Divine Crossing Tower, New York
entries.description: The incomplete yet awe-inspiring crossing tower of this cathedral — the largest Gothic-style church in the world — has been under construction since 1892, earning it the nickname 'St. John the Unfinished.'

entries.title: Old Ship Church, Hingham
entries.description: Built in 1681, Old Ship Church is the oldest continuously operating church in the United States. Its unique roof structure, resembling an inverted ship's hull, was crafted by local shipwrights.
